18: ho logos ho tou staurou (ho logos ho tou staurou, 'the word of the cross'): Paul's phrase is deliberate — not 'the theology of the cross' or 'the doctrine of the atonement' but the logos itself, the very message. moria (mōria, foolishness): Greco-Roman rhetoric valued wisdom (sophia) displayed through elegant argumentation (logos). Execution on a cross (stauros) was the most shameful death possible — reserved for slaves, pirates, and enemies of the state (Cicero Pro Rabirio 5.16: 'let the very word cross be far from the body, eyes, and ears of Roman citizens'). That this is the power (dynamis) of God for those being saved is Paul's inversion of all Greco-Roman honor-shame discourse.
24: Christon Theou dynamin kai Theou sophian (Christon Theou dynamin kai Theou sophian): 'Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.' Paul here makes Christ the personification of both divine attributes — power and wisdom are not merely Christ's qualities but identified with his person. This comes after the Corinthian division over rhetorical wisdom (1 Corinthians 1:12: some follow Apollo's eloquence, some Peter, some Paul). Paul's response: the divided parties are competing over what is no competition — Christ alone is Wisdom, and that wisdom is cruciform.
30: sophias kai dikaiosynes kai hagiasmos kai apolytrosis: 'wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption.' The four nouns in asyndeton give Christ four forensic/cultic roles. Dikaiosyne (righteousness) covers the forensic standing; hagiasmos (sanctification/holiness) the cultic status; apolytrosis (redemption/release) the commercial-legal deliverance from bondage. That these are all said to be 'from God' (apo theou) makes clear they are received, not achieved. Paul is attacking the Corinthians' pride in human wisdom by showing that what they need — status, holiness, freedom — comes only from Christ.
1: κλητὸς ἀπόστολος (klētos apostolos) — called apostle. The adjective klētos (called, invited) places Paul's apostleship entirely in the divine initiative: he did not choose the role; he was called into it. The same word describes the Corinthians in 1 Corinthians 1:2 (klētois hagiois — called saints). Paul's apostolic authority and the community's identity rest on the same ground: divine calling, not human qualification.
2: ἡγιασμένοις (hēgiasmenois) — perfect passive participle: those who have been sanctified and remain in that sanctified state. The perfect tense is not merely historical; it describes an ongoing condition that results from a past completed action. The community is already sanctified — a state given in Christ — before any ethical transformation is discussed. This established holiness is then the basis for the ethical demands that follow in the letter.
3: χάρις καὶ εἰρήνη (charis kai eirēnē) — grace and peace. The standard Pauline greeting combines the Greek epistolary greeting (chairein, greetings/rejoice, modified to charis) with the Jewish shalom. The combination is not merely stylistic; charis names the mode of divine action (unmerited favor) and eirēnē names the resulting state (covenant wholeness). Both flow "from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" — a coordination of sources that implicitly equates Jesus with the Father as a source of divine blessing.
4: εὐχαριστῶ (eucharistō) — present tense: I am always (pantote) thanking my God. The continuous present in a thanksgiving formula was a conventional letter-opener, but Paul's formula is theologically dense: the thanksgiving is specifically "because of the grace of God given you in Christ Jesus." The gift is not Paul's teaching or their response but God's prior grace.
5: λόγῳ... γνώσει (in all speech and all knowledge) — both nouns are datives of sphere (enriched in the sphere of speech; enriched in the sphere of knowledge). The very gifts Paul will challenge (rhetorical skill, knowledge) are acknowledged as real gifts from God. The problem is not the gifts themselves but the Corinthians' misunderstanding of their source and purpose.
6: τὸ μαρτύριον τοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐβεβαιώθη (the testimony of Christ was confirmed/made firm) — aorist passive: a completed confirming act. The verb bebaioō is a legal term for formally ratifying or guaranteeing a contract or legal status. God's confirming of the gospel testimony in the community (through the charismatic gifts of 1 Corinthians 1:5-7) is a legal ratification, not merely experiential validation.
7: ἀπεκδεχομένους (apekdechomenous) — present active participle: those who are eagerly awaiting. The prefix apo-ek- intensifies the waiting: earnest, sustained expectation. The community is defined by eschatological posture: they are "waiting" people. The apokalypsis (revelation/unveiling) they await is Christ's definitive disclosure at the Parousia.
8: βεβαιώσει (bebaiōsei) — future indicative: he will confirm/make firm. The same root as 1 Corinthians 1:6 (bebaioō): the God who confirmed the testimony in the past (1 Corinthians 1:6) will also confirm the community at the end. The blameless (anenkletous) state is not ethical achievement but the forensic verdict of the judge — they will be found without charge before the tribunal.
9: πιστὸς ὁ θεός (pistos ho theos) — God is faithful. The adjective pistos shares its root with pistis (faith/trust); YHWH's faithfulness is his character of covenant reliability that makes trust in him possible. The calling was through God's faithfulness (not Corinthian deserving), and the sustaining to the end will be through the same faithfulness. The entire argument for the community's security rests not on their spiritual status but on God's character.
10: παρακαλῶ (parakalō) — I appeal, I urge. The present tense marks an urgent ongoing appeal; this is not a polite request but the technical language of pastoral exhortation. The root para-kaleō (calling alongside) is also the root of paraklētos (advocate, helper). The appeal is σχίσματα (schismata — tears, splits): Paul uses the language of fabric being torn. The community is not merely divided in opinion; it is being structurally ripped apart.
11: ἐριδες (erides) — contentions, rivalries. The plural of eris (strife personified as a goddess in Greek tradition); the term describes the kind of passionate competitive conflict that characterizes rhetoric competitions and legal disputes. The Corinthians' factional disputes carry the energy of public rhetorical combat.
12: ἐγὼ δέ εἰμι (I am of...) — the emphatic personal pronoun egō gives each faction member a proud, assertive voice: I belong to Paul, I belong to Apollos. The formula echoes the Hellenistic philosophical school-allegiance language ("I am of Plato's school"). The Corinthians are treating the gospel message as a philosophical school with competing teachers, each school having a distinct character to which adherents belong.
13: μεμέρισται ὁ Χριστός (has Christ been divided?) — perfect passive indicative used interrogatively with expected negative answer. The perfect tense is pointed: has Christ been and-remained-in-a-state-of-division? The absurdity Paul highlights is theological: the divided community implies a divided Christ, which is impossible. The factions have made an ontological claim about Christ's unity without realizing it.
14: Κρίσπον καὶ Γάϊον (Crispus and Gaius) — Paul recalls the names of those he personally baptized from memory, noting his relief that he baptized so few. Crispus is likely the synagogue ruler of Acts 18:8; Gaius is mentioned in Romans 16:23. The parenthetical quality of this verse shows Paul working through his argument without a prepared text.
15: ἐβαπτίσθητε (you were baptized) — aorist passive: the single completed event of baptism. Paul's concern is the preposition: baptized into whose name? Baptism in the name of someone meant initiation into that person's sphere of authority and identity (cf. baptism into Moses in 1 Corinthians 10:2). Being baptized into Paul's name would have been a catastrophic misrepresentation of whose people the Corinthians were.
16: The parenthetical aside about Stephanas's household — "beyond that I don't know if I baptized anyone else" — is a rare glimpse of Paul composing in real time, correcting his statement as he writes. It demonstrates that the letter is not a polished rhetorical piece but genuine correspondence. The spontaneous correction also makes the theological argument that follows more credible: Paul is not trying to disclaim baptizing people; it simply was not his primary mission.
17: οὐκ ἐν σοφίᾳ λόγου (not in wisdom of speech) — the genitive lōgou (of word/speech) specifies: not wisdom in the abstract but wisdom-as-rhetorical-skill. Sophia logou was the technical term for the rhetorical eloquence of the Sophists — persuasion through elegant speech. Paul's mission was to preach euangelion in such a way that the cross would not be emptied of its power (kenōthē, aorist passive subjunctive: lest it be emptied).
19: γέγραπται γάρ (for it stands written) — perfect passive: the standard citation formula, using the perfect to indicate that the written word stands in permanent force. The text cited (Isaiah 29:14) is not merely a historical statement; it is a permanent divine declaration that remains in effect. The perfect gegraptai is how Paul marks the ongoing authority of all his scriptural citations.
20: ποῦ σοφός (where is the wise person?) — the triple rhetorical question (pou... pou... pou) is a rhetorical taunt. Γραμματεύς (grammateus = scribe, secretary, teacher of the law) locates the wisdom-failure in the Jewish scribal tradition specifically; συζητητής τοῦ αἰῶνος τούτου (debater of this age) locates it in Hellenistic philosophical disputation. Both are exposed as futile by the cross. The phrase ὁ αἰὼν οὗτος (this age) introduces the apocalyptic two-age schema.
21: εὐδόκησεν ὁ θεὸς (God was pleased, God decided) — aorist active: God made a single decisive choice. The verb eudokeō connotes both pleasure and purposeful decision. God's sovereign decision to save through the preaching that the world considers foolish (mōria tou kērygmatos — the foolishness OF the proclamation, not a foolish proclamation) is the ground for the cross-strategy. The genitive expresses the content that renders the proclamation foolish in human estimation.
22: σημεῖα αἰτοῦσιν... σοφίαν ζητοῦσιν (demand signs... seek wisdom) — the verbs carry distinct tones: aiteō (demand, request with expectation of receiving) vs. zēteō (seek, investigate). Jews demand visible divine attestation; Greeks pursue intellectual coherence. Both are seeking a gospel that fits their existing criteria for what divine action must look like.
23:
Χριστὸν ἐσταυρωμένον (Christ crucified) — the perfect passive participle estaurōmenon is crucial: not "Christ who was crucified" (aorist) but "Christ who has been crucified and remains in the crucified state." The crucifixion is not merely a past event in Christ's biography; it is his permanent defining identity. The Messiah is permanently defined by the cross.

σκάνδαλον (skandalon = stumbling block, trap) — more than offense; the word denotes a mechanism that causes someone to fall. The cross is not merely culturally offensive to Jewish ears but structurally incompatible with Jewish messianic expectation of a conquering king.
25: τὸ μωρὸν τοῦ θεοῦ... τὸ ἀσθενὲς τοῦ θεοῦ (the foolish of God, the weak of God) — these are neuter substantives, not adjectival descriptions of specific things but abstractions: "the-foolish-thing-that-is-God's" and "the-weak-thing-that-is-God's." The genitive identifies these as belonging to God's mode of action, not as genuine attributes. What appears as foolishness in God's action surpasses all human wisdom in its effectiveness.
26: Βλέπετε γὰρ τὴν κλῆσιν ὑμῶν (consider your calling) — the imperative blepete (look at, consider) directs attention to the empirical evidence: the community itself is the argument. Klēsis (calling, vocation) is the same word-group as the klētos of 1 Corinthians 1:1; the Corinthians' own existence as a called community proves the point about divine selection criteria.
27: ἐξελέξατο (exelexato) — aorist middle: God chose for himself. The middle voice emphasizes the divine self-involvement in the act of election; God chose these for his own purposes. The threefold repetition (exelexato... exelexato... exelexato) creates an anaphora that insists on divine election as the exclusive explanation for the community's existence.
28: τὰ μὴ ὄντα (the things that are not) — present participle of eimi with the negative: literally "the non-beings." Paul's language touches on creation-from-nothing; the God who calls things-that-are-not into existence (Romans 4:17) is the same God who calls the social nobodies of Corinth into his purpose. The community's existence is a creational act.
29: ὅπως μὴ καυχήσηται πᾶσα σάρξ (so that no flesh might boast) — purpose clause with aorist subjunctive: the specific aim of the divine selection strategy. Pasa sarx (all flesh) is a Hebrew-Bible expression (kol basar) denoting humanity as mortal, creaturely, limited — the opposite of the divine. The prohibition on boasting before God is absolute: no creaturely ground for pride before the Creator.
31: ὁ καυχώμενος ἐν κυρίῳ καυχάσθω (let the one who boasts boast in the Lord) — the present imperative kauchāsthō: an ongoing rule, not a single act. The only legitimate boasting (kauchēsis) is in the Lord — specifically, in knowing YHWH who exercises covenant-love, justice, and righteousness (Jeremiah 9:24). Paul's citation replaces all forms of human boasting (wisdom, skill, status, spiritual gifts) with a single legitimate form: covenant-knowledge of the God who acts in the cross.
